grammar.usage: Use the past simple/continuous to refer to present/future situations, and the past perfect simple/continuous to refer to hypothetical past situations.
grammar.example: I'd buy this top if I had more cash on me.

grammar.usage: Use wish/if only with past tense for present/future wishes, past perfect for past wishes, would to complain or criticize, could for ability/permission.
grammar.example: If only Henry would get a haircut!

grammar.usage: Use although, though, even though, in spite of, despite, however, nevertheless, while, whereas to express contrasting ideas.
grammar.example: Although I wrote myself a note, I still forgot to call George tonight.

bullets: Sometimes we use the past simple and past continuous to refer to present or future situations. This is called the unreal past.
Sometimes we use the past perfect simple and past perfect continuous to refer to hypothetical past situations that did not happen.

rows: Second conditional
To describe hypothetical present/future situations
I'd buy this top if I had more cash on me.
Suppose / What if / Imagine + past tense
To describe situations that are unlikely to happen (giả sử / chuyện gì sẽ xảy ra / tưởng tượng xem...)

EX: I know it's probably not going to happen but suppose / imagine I became a famous super model.
EX: What if you were accepted into art college?
Suppose / What if / Imagine + present tense
To describe situations that are likely to happen
EX: What if you are accepted into art college?
Would rather (+ not)
Would rather + Vbare (to express preference for ourselves)
EX: I'd rather not come to this class tomorrow.
Would rather + subject + past tense
To express preference for someone else's actions
EX: I'd rather you didn't come with me to the fashion show tomorrow.
It's high (/about) time
It's high (/about) time + subject + past tense (to express that something should be done now or is overdue)
EX: It's high (/about) time that hats came back in fashion.
Polite questions
Use past simple/continuous to ask questions politely
EX: Did you want me to send you our new catalogue when it comes out?
Wish / If only + past tense
To express present or future wishes
EX: If only / I wish I had something to wear tonight.
Wishes about the present / future

Wish / If only + V2/ed (/ was/ were + Ving)
Used for things we want to be different now or in the future.
EX: I wish I was studying history of fashion instead of biology.
Wishes about the past

Wish / If only + had V3/ed (/ had been + Ving)
Used for regrets about past events.
EX: If only I'd known Burton's was having a sale last week.
Criticizing or complaining

Wish / if only + would
Used to criticize someone or complain about a situation now/future.
EX: If only Henry would get a haircut!
Ability / permission

Wish / if only + could
Used to express ability or permission to do something now/future.
EX: I wish I could fit into these jeans.
Desire / formal requests

Wish + to Vbare
Used to express a formal desire or request.
EX: I wish to try on this ball gown.

richGrammar.title: C) NHỮNG CỤM TỪ DIỄN TẢ SỰ ĐỐI NGHỊCH

rows: Although / Though / Even Though + S + V, clause
EX: Although I wrote myself a note to remind me, I still forgot to call George tonight.
S + V, although / though / even though + clause.
EX: The kids failed to find the tennis ball, although they had looked everywhere.

blocks.content: 2. NOTES ON ALTHOUGH, THOUGH, EVEN THOUGH

bullets: **Even though:** implies stronger contrast than although or though.
**Though:** more informal than although or even though.

**Though at the end:** 'Though' can be placed at the end of a sentence.
EX: I wore a hat. I got sunburn, though.

rows: In spite of / Despite + Ving, clause
EX: In spite of / Despite having been sure (that) she would win the competition, Sally came third.
In spite of / despite + the fact (that) + clause, clause.
EX: In spite of / Despite the fact (that) he was exhausted, Mike kept on climbing.
In spite of / despite + N, S + V
EX: In spite of / Despite the storm, over ten thousand people took part in the marathon.

blocks.content: 4. NOTES ON IN SPITE OF / DESPITE

bullets:
**Position:** 'In spite of' or 'despite' can be placed in the middle of a sentence.
EX: Doug wasn't shortlisted for the job in spite of / despite a great CV.

**Prior Action:** Use the structure 'in spite of / despite + Ving' to describe an action that happened before the result.
EX: I got sunburn in spite of / despite having worn a hat.

**Subject Agreement:** In the structure 'In spite of / despite + Ving', the subject of the main clause must be the person performing the V-ing action.
✗ My sunburn was terrible in spite of / despite wearing a hat. (Incorrect because my sunburn didn't wear a hat!)
✓ I had a terrible sunburn in spite of / despite wearing a hat.

rows: Clause 1. However / Nevertheless, clause 2
EX: Most Egyptologists believe that the Sphinx is about 4000 years old. Some geologists, however/nevertheless, argue that it must be at least 14,000 years old.

bullets:
**Positioning:** 'However' or 'nevertheless' can be placed at the start, middle, or end of a sentence.
EX1: The fashion show was expensive to put on. However / Nevertheless, it did make a profit.
EX2: The fashion show was expensive to put on. It did make a profit, however / nevertheless.
EX3: The fashion show was expensive to put on. It did, however / nevertheless, make a profit.

rows:
While / whereas + S + V, S + V
Used to show differences between two facts or ideas.
EX: While / whereas jeans are worn by both men and women, blouses are worn only by women.
S + V, while / whereas + S + V
EX: Blouses are worn only by women, while / whereas jeans are worn by both men and women.

richGrammar.title: D) MỘT SỐ CÁCH NÓI "DÙ CHO..." DẠNG CAO CẤP

rows: Adj/adv + as/though + S + V, Clause.

EX1: Sad as/though it is, unemployment is unlikely to go down this year.
EX2: Much as/though Chloe didn't agree with the manager's decision, she was forced to accept it.
However + adj/adv + S + V, Clause.
EX: However poor he is, he still studies well.
No matter how + adj/adv + S + V, Clause.
EX: No matter how high the salary was, the young girl stopped working.
No matter what/ when/ why/ who/ whose/ where/ if... (+ N) + S + V, Clause.
EX: No matter what their score in the competition is, all the children will receive a prize

vocabulary.meaning: on the stage where models walk to show new clothes
vocabulary.example: The models strutted confidently on the catwalk showing the winter collection.

vocabulary.meaning: a business, shop, or organization, or the act of starting one
vocabulary.example: The new restaurant has already become a popular establishment in the city.

vocabulary.meaning: to involve yourself in a situation where you are not wanted or needed
vocabulary.example: I wish you wouldn't interfere in my personal life.

vocabulary.meaning: a period of rapid economic growth and prosperity
vocabulary.example: The country experienced a major economic boom in the late 1990s.

vocabulary.meaning: a disadvantage or problem that makes something less attractive
vocabulary.example: The main drawback of the new job is the long commute.

vocabulary.meaning: the state of depending on or trusting someone or something
vocabulary.example: The company is trying to reduce its reliance on foreign suppliers.

vocabulary.meaning: to break suddenly with a sharp noise, or to gãy, đứt, bể
vocabulary.example: The branch snapped under the weight of the snow.
She was in an upbeat mood after receiving the good news.

vocabulary.meaning: great admiration, honor, and praise that you earn, or great beauty
vocabulary.example: The athletes competed for the glory of their country.

vocabulary.meaning: a metal fastener used to join the ends of a belt or strap
vocabulary.example: He fastened the buckle of his belt before leaving.

vocabulary.meaning: a strap or support used to hold something in place, or straps over shoulders to hold trousers up (braces)
vocabulary.example: He wore braces to hold his trousers up.

vocabulary.meaning: a small, round piece of glass, wood, or plastic with a hole through it, used for making jewelry
vocabulary.example: She wore a necklace made of colorful wooden beads.

vocabulary.meaning: clothing designed to be worn on the beach
vocabulary.example: The shop sells swimsuits, sunglasses, and other beachwear.

vocabulary.meaning: the state of not being sensible, realistic, or easy to do
vocabulary.example: The impracticality of the plan became obvious when we looked at the budget.

vocabulary.meaning: extremely large in size, amount, or strength, or very great
vocabulary.example: The team has made tremendous progress over the last month.
The book was so dull that I couldn't finish reading it.
